hard.next_step: 50% confidence 02 JUL - Have requested an update on the process 12 JUN - Luke Davies says decision will be delayed a bit further, have offered assistance to help any technical or design hurdles. Concerned about the lack of engagement with us. 9th Jun IT have delayed the selection process 2-3 weeks 25 MAY - Advised decision will be delayed as requirements are reviewed, I am pushing them as to what we can do to help them get through this and get started...will keep trying. Said decision might be delayed 2 months. 11 MAY - Delayed a few weeks due to additional requirements being considered, mainly on ERP integration. They are sending questions this week and we will schedule an integration session. Still hopeful of Q1 close- Carl on standby to F2F visit when needed. 30 APR - Next step is decision. We provided scenario based demo session from RFP, will get feedback on Friday 1st May 20 APR - Response submitted and demo session booked for next week, 29 APR 13 APR - Working through RFP and we have some gaps in obligation tracking but they still want Zycus to respond and to see demo of what we can do now and understand what we will commit to adding, within 12 months. 31 MAR - RFP reviewed and YES to respond, working through it now. 25 MAR - PM has been told to issue RFP to both shortlisted vendors (he was trying to avoid this) Hopes to send it out by end of week. 16 Mar - Final commercials submitted- preference is for Zycus and have offered LOI in Mar but we are pushing very hard for contract signing and close 13 Mar - Been shortlisted. they wanted another session, going over key use cases. Aiming for decision and contracts done by end of mar 04 March - Further deep dive provided this week and responses to security requests. Next step is selection in final two, by mid month. Am pushing for an end of quarter special deal. 26 Feb - Running another demo on their use case, high value energy contracts, Monday 2nd March. Looking to speed decision process. High chance to win, however timelines to execute are narrowing 10th Feb- small opportunity with Zycus well preferred- waiting on internal green light- 1 higher priority project in motion 02 FEB - No progress or urgency on this yet, says it will be a priority within next 8 weeks 15th JAN - No progress at SFV this week, does not seem to be anything we can do at present, he is waiting for other team members input as they come back from summer leave. Suggested reconnect on 22nd for progress check and next steps 13th Jan- Positive movement and likely to Zycus 05 JAN - Expecting to re-engage next week 12 DEC - We have responded to the EOI. It's a smaller opportunity but could be a good entry to NSW Government. They will respond with questions, hopefully prior to Christmas. Zycus team to evaluate the EOI and check if we can match the requirement and participate in the tender process with best pricing
hard.ais_why:
• Per the 11 May 2026 rep note: 'Delayed a few weeks due to additional requirements being considered, mainly on ERP integration. They are sending questions this week and we will schedule an integration session.' On 13 Apr 2026: 'Working through RFP and we have some gaps in obligation tracking but they still want Zycus to respond and to see demo of what we can do now and understand what we will commit to adding, within 12 months.' Buyer concerns are ERP integration and obligation tracking ‚Äî pure traditional CLM.
• On 26 Feb 2026: 'Running another demo on their use case, high value energy contracts.' The deal is anchored in high-value energy contracts (CLM use case) ‚Äî not AI capability. Merlin and Merlin Intake appear in product scope but never in dated rep dialogue, RFP commentary, or demo focus across the 2,380-char NextStep.
• Confidence is Medium because the NextStep is moderately rich but covers a discrete CLM evaluation rather than a broad S2P. The absence of any AI keyword (AI, Merlin, agentic, GenAI) across the entire history despite Merlin/Merlin Intake bundling in Products suggests Zycus has positioned AI optionally; buyer is not pulling for it.

north_star_verdict.headline: Zycus is the buyer-preferred finalist of two on this $110k CLM deal, but the decision has stalled three times in seven weeks and the buyer is now reopening requirements. Close date 31 Aug is not credible without a locked decision timeline.
north_star_verdict.math: Created 27 Nov 2025 (221 days old). In Shortlisted for ~109 days (entered ~19 Mar per Next Step 13 Mar). Close date has slipped four times: 25 Apr -> 15 May -> 15 Jun -> 30 Jul -> 31 Aug. Three decision delays: 11 May (ERP integration), 25 May (two-month warning), 9 Jun (IT delayed 2-3 weeks). Last two-way buyer touch 2 Jul (Luke Davies email: finishing requirements review, hope to re-engage shortly). At current pace (no decision milestone crossed since 16 Mar LOI offer, budget owner never engaged, obligation features on 12-month roadmap), decision + contracting + InfoSec + close in 56 days is not credible; a 60-90 day slip is likely.
evidence: Shortlisted since ~19 Mar; today 6 Jul, ~109 days in stage with no advance to Vendor Selected or Negotiation. Source: Next Step 13 Mar, StageName.
Three decision delays in seven weeks: 11 May (ERP integration, a few weeks), 25 May (two-month delay while requirements reviewed), 9 Jun (IT delayed selection 2-3 weeks). Source: Next Step.

Buyer reopened requirements on 2 Jul: Luke Davies emailed 'We are just finishing our review of the original requirements, and hope to re-engage shortly. Our PM will be in contact in due course on next steps.' Source: Task 2 Jul.
Commercials submitted and LOI offered 16 Mar; 110+ days later no contract signed and no commercial/contracting motion. Source: Next Step 16 Mar.

Budget owner Nick Middleton (Deputy General Counsel and Head of Contract Management) has never appeared in a call, Next Step, or task across the cycle. Source: OpportunityContactRole; call attendee logs 2 Mar, 29 Apr.
Best Case is not defensible: mid-stage stall, budget owner unengaged, scope cut from 139k to 110k, obligation dashboard and escalation on 12-month roadmap. Honest category: Pipeline.

metrics.narrative: No quantified value case exists after 221 days. On the 2 Mar and 29 Apr calls the buyer (Luke Davies, Operations Analyst) articulated the pain qualitatively: 'At the moment, everything we do is manual. Excel in our brains trying to tackle these' (2 Mar), and stressed the stakes on 29 Apr: 'we can't afford for any of these to be overdue because we'll be in breach' with 'severe penalties... probably financial penalties'. That names real breach-and-penalty exposure on high-value energy contracts, but no figure was ever put to it: no FTE hours lost to manual obligation tracking, no dollar value of a missed obligation, no cycle-time or ROI target. The absence of a number is why a 'small opportunity' (Next Step 10 Feb) keeps losing to '1 higher priority project in motion'. Move: model the cost of manual obligation tracking and breach risk with the contract-management team to give Nick Middleton a financial anchor.
sources: Avoma Zycus CLM Demo 29 Apr 2026 (Shane Capps, Luke Davies)

economic_buyer.narrative: Nick Middleton, Deputy General Counsel and Head of Contract Management, is the mapped business/budget owner per his Salesforce contact role and title, and is the natural approver for a contract-lifecycle purchase. He is, however, entirely passive: he has never joined a call (2 Mar, 29 Apr attendees were Luke Davies, Shane Capps, Ahmad Salar, Chitra Shende and a compliance lead), never appears in a Next Step, and has not sponsored the decision through three delays. Day-to-day the procurement is run by Luke Davies (Operations Analyst), who leads the evaluation but lacks budget authority ('waiting on internal green light', Next Step 10 Feb). So the EB is identified but unengaged, which is the core mid-stage risk here. Move: use Luke Davies to broker a direct working session with Nick Middleton to confirm budget ownership and the decision timeline.
sources: OpportunityContactRole: Nick Middleton, Deputy General Counsel and Head of Contract Management

decision_criteria.narrative: The evaluation criteria are explicit and evidenced. The buyer ran an RFI (2025), then an RFP to two shortlisted vendors, then a scenario-based demo on 29 Apr against three buyer-supplied use cases: (1) tracking obligations plus automated alerts on long-term energy contracts, (2) contract variations with approval workflow and e-signature, and (3) ingestion of supplier reporting data. The number-one criterion is obligation management with escalation: Shane Capps (IT Manager) pressed 'can you work workflow in escalation... we need to make sure enough people know when something's falling behind' (29 Apr). Secondary criteria are ERP/business-systems integration, SSO/security (Shane Capps confirmed 29 Apr he was comfortable on security and SSO), and configurability. Roadmap timing is itself a criterion: the buyer repeatedly asked when the obligation dashboard and escalation features would ship. Move: get the obligation/escalation roadmap and integration approach in writing to close the criteria that are still open.
sources: Avoma Zycus CLM Demo 29 Apr 2026 (Shane Capps, Luke Davies)

decision_process.narrative: The process is a New South Wales public-sector-style sourcing run: EOI (Dec 2025) -> RFI response -> shortlist to two (26 Mar) -> RFP to both finalists (issued late Mar, the PM was told to run a two-vendor RFP he had tried to avoid) -> scenario demos (29 Apr) -> selection. The call sequence shows a stable but junior committee: Luke Davies drives, Shane Capps gates IT/security, Ahmad Salar and Chitra Shende cover contract management, with a compliance lead present; no senior sponsor has joined at any stage. An internal PM controls the timeline and is the source of the repeated slips ('IT have delayed the selection process 2-3 weeks', 9 Jun; 'Our PM will be in contact in due course', 2 Jul). Approval chain above Luke Davies (through Nick Middleton) is inferred, not confirmed. Move: ask Luke Davies to map who signs off and secure a written decision date from the PM.
sources: SF Next Step 12 Dec, 26 Mar, 25 Mar, 9 Jun

paper_process.narrative: This is a single-module iContract-led CLM buy (with iManage, iRisk, Merlin/Merlin Intake bundled), so contracting is Order Form plus SOW rather than a full-suite MSA. Commercials were submitted and an LOI offered on 16 Mar, and Zycus positioned fixed-cost implementation via its own global delivery team. Luke Dougherty offered on 29 Apr to write the obligation-feature roadmap dates into the agreement ('This would all be written into your agreement... so you have the assurance that the functionality is coming'), which becomes a contractual commitment to track. Beyond the LOI there is no visible legal/redline, procurement-onboarding, or signature motion, and no PO is expected for an Australian public-body buy of this size. Move: convert the LOI into an agreed Order Form and a roadmap-dated SOW to create a signature path.

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is specific and owner-named. Scheme Financial Vehicle is a new NSW financial intermediary managing complex, high-value renewable-energy contracts entirely by hand: Luke Davies said 'everything we do is manual. Excel in our brains' and 'as we're growing, we need a better way of doing it' (2 Mar). The acute pain is obligation breach exposure: Shane Capps stressed on 29 Apr that overdue obligations would put them 'in breach' with 'severe penalties', and Chitra Shende flagged time-barred clauses ('as soon as we receive a notice, we have got, say, 40 to respond'). Pain owners are the contract-management function (Nick Middleton, Ahmad Salar, Chitra Shende) with IT (Shane Capps) owning integration/escalation. Confirmed pain: manual tracking, breach risk, no escalation. Inferred pain: reporting burden from suppliers. Move: tie the ROI case directly to breach-penalty avoidance.
sources: Avoma Zycus CLM Demo 29 Apr 2026 (Shane Capps, Chitra Shende)

champion.narrative: Luke Davies (Operations Analyst, buyer-side, appears in tasks/emails and both calls though not on the formal contact-role list) is the working champion: he drives the evaluation, shares sample data, praised Zycus as the preference on 16 Mar, and on 12 Jun 'offered assistance to help any technical or design hurdles' while voicing 'concern about the lack of engagement with us' from his own side. That concern is the tell: he advocates but cannot force a decision and has no confirmed access to the budget owner. No senior champion (Nick Middleton or above) is engaged, and Shane Capps is a neutral gatekeeper, not an advocate. Champion strength is weak-to-developing and at risk. What has developed: consistent single-thread advocacy and demo access. What is missing: authority, a path to the EB, and the ability to hold a decision date. Move: coach Luke Davies to open Nick Middleton and co-own a mutual close plan.

competition.narrative: This is a two-vendor final: 'PM has been told to issue RFP to both shortlisted vendors' (25 Mar), with Ironclad the named rival (SF competitor field). Zycus was preferred after commercials on 16 Mar, but the real threat is not Ironclad winning, it is no-decision inertia: the buyer manages everything manually today, has a higher-priority internal project (10 Feb), and keeps reopening requirements rather than choosing. Zycus's wedge over Ironclad, a document/CLM-centric player, is deeper obligation tracking for high-value energy contracts plus a native integration layer and full source-to-pay optionality. The vulnerability is that the buyer's top asks, an obligation dashboard and rule-based escalation, are Zycus roadmap (12 months out), not live, which Ironclad can exploit. Move: run a competitive review against Ironclad and neutralise the roadmap gap with a written commitment.

deal_movement.summary: Momentum is negative. After the 29 Apr scenario demo the deal slipped three times and reopened requirements on 2 Jul; the close date has moved four times to 31 Aug and the amount was cut from 139k to 110k. Last two-way buyer touch was Luke Davies' 2 Jul email deferring re-engagement.

items.change: Buyer (Luke Davies) emailed that they are finishing review of original requirements and 'hope to re-engage shortly', PM to make contact on next steps
Buyer signalled a further decision delay and Luke Davies voiced concern about lack of engagement; Zycus offered technical/design assistance
IT delayed the selection process 2-3 weeks
Buyer warned decision may be delayed up to two months while requirements are reviewed
Delivered scenario-based demo on three buyer use cases (obligation tracking, variations, supplier reporting)

competitive_position.summary: The single strongest current threat is no-decision inertia, not a vendor: the buyer runs everything manually today, has a higher-priority internal project (10 Feb) and has reopened requirements three times since 11 May. Ironclad is the one active vendor rival in a two-vendor RFP final (25 Mar), a document/CLM-centric player Zycus leads on obligation-tracking depth and integration; Zycus was the stated preference on 16 Mar but has not converted in 110+ days. The competing internal project remains a live priority drag.

competitors.quote: At the moment, everything we do is manual. Excel in our brains trying to tackle these.
competitors.source: Avoma SFV demo 2 Mar 2026 (Luke Davies); Next Step 25 May, 9 Jun, 2 Jul (repeated delays)
competitors.how_we_win: Quantify the breach-penalty and hours cost of manual Excel tracking so the status quo becomes untenable and tie a go-live date to an energy-contract obligation deadline.

competitors.name: Ironclad
competitors.quote: PM has been told to issue RFP to both shortlisted vendors (he was trying to avoid this).

competitors.how_we_win: Ironclad is document/CLM-centric; win on deeper obligation tracking for high-value energy contracts, native ERP integration layer, and full source-to-pay optionality.

competitors.name: Competing internal project
competitors.quote: Small opportunity with Zycus well preferred- waiting on internal green light- 1 higher priority project in motion.
competitors.how_we_win: Attach the CLM go-live to a compelling event (energy-contract obligation deadlines) so it earns priority over the competing initiative.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Zycus fits the core CLM and variations criteria well, but the two features the buyer cares most about, an obligation dashboard and rule-based escalation, are roadmap (12 months out), and ERP integration is still being scoped by the buyer.

items.criterion: Obligation capture and automated alerts on long-term energy contracts
items.quote: against each of these obligations, you can define a task... the solution would also have the capability to send out calendar invites so that the deadlines do not get missed

items.criterion: Obligation dashboard (per-user task view of pending/overdue/at-risk obligations)
items.quote: We are building it. It's in the road map, for the next year. It's not live in the solution, but I do have a wireframe

items.criterion: Rule-based escalation when an obligation is overdue or about to be
items.quote: escalation management and rule based escalation is something that we are building on currently

items.criterion: Contract variations with approval workflow, versioning and e-signature
items.quote: you can just click on the create variation button... you've got that whole revision audit history
there is one manual intervention that would be required... you have to actually go ahead, export that entire page, pull that into the contract report
defining the potential integration is a part of why we are spending more time to refine our requirements
items.source: Task 21 May (Luke Davies); Shane Capps confirmed SSO/security comfort 29 Apr

items.requirement: Confirm rule-based escalation for overdue/at-risk obligations
items.quote: Can you work workflow in escalation... we make sure enough people know when something's falling behind or about to fall behind.

items.requirement: Provide the obligation-dashboard delivery date in writing
items.quote: You say it's on the road map. Do you have any indication of timing?

items.requirement: Define the ERP and business-systems integration approach
items.quote: Defining the potential integration is a part of why we are spending more time to refine our requirements.

items.deliverable: Put the obligation dashboard and escalation roadmap dates into the agreement for Scheme Financial Vehicle
items.grounding_quote: This would all be written into your agreement to say that the dates of this is coming so you have the assurance that the functionality is coming as well.

items.deliverable: Bring a Zycus integration/migration consultant onto a call to work the ERP integration requirements
items.grounding_quote: he can bring one of our integration / migration consultants on to a call to discuss requirements

items.deliverable: Send the integration questions the buyer committed to on 11 May
items.grounding_quote: They are sending questions this week and we will schedule an integration session.

items.deliverable: Confirm the decision timeline and next steps promised by the buyer PM
items.grounding_quote: Our PM will be in contact in due course on next steps.

items.quote: we can't afford for any of these to be overdue because we'll be in breach... severe penalties

items.area: Economic buyer engagement (Nick Middleton budget authority unconfirmed)

items.area: Contact-role hygiene: Luke Davies leads the buy but is not on the OpportunityContactRole list and no roles are populated

best_practice_check.summary: A preferred but stalling two-vendor final where the biggest threat is no-decision inertia and the budget owner has never engaged. The highest-leverage levers are neutralising the roadmap gap and reaching Nick Middleton.
flags: Break the no-decision stall: convert the 2 Jul 're-engage shortly' into a fixed decision date and a mutual close plan owned with Luke Davies.
Reach the budget owner: Nick Middleton (Head of Contract Management) has never engaged in 221 days; a preferred $110k deal cannot close without him.
Neutralise the roadmap gap: get the obligation-dashboard and escalation dates written into the agreement so Ironclad cannot exploit the 12-month wait.

items.name: Nick Middleton
items.title: Deputy General Counsel & Head of Contract Management
items.risk: Mapped budget/business owner but never engaged across the cycle; decision has stalled three times with no senior sponsorship.

champion_strength.summary: Luke Davies (Operations Analyst) is a consistent single-thread advocate who named Zycus the preference on 16 Mar and offers technical help, but on 12 Jun voiced concern about his own organisation's lack of engagement, exposing that he cannot force a decision or reach the budget owner. Developing at best, and at risk while the decision stalls.
champion_strength.source: SF Next Step 16 Mar, 12 Jun; Task 2 Jul; Avoma 2 Mar and 29 Apr

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Merlin and Merlin Intake are bundled and were demoed (conversational obligation queries, Gen-AI contract discovery), but the buyer is anchored on traditional CLM and obligation tracking and never engaged the AI story. Pushing AI harder is unlikely to move this buyer; the win is on obligation depth and integration, not AI.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS 3.0 / Negative is accurate: across the entire history the buyer never raised AI, Merlin, agentic or GenAI on their own; Merlin Intake and Gen-AI discovery were rep-led and drew no pull. The deal is a discrete CLM/obligation-tracking evaluation, not an AI play.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement is cooling: the last two-way buyer exchange (2 Jul) was a soft defer, following three delays and a slipped close date. The deal is preferred but drifting.

Held below a confident number because the buyer's top asks, an obligation dashboard and rule-based escalation, are Zycus roadmap 12 months out, not live (Amisha, 29 Apr), a gap Ironclad, a CLM specialist, can exploit.

Last real buyer exchange was Luke Davies' 2 Jul email 'finishing our review of the original requirements, hope to re-engage shortly', a soft defer rather than a next step.

But there has been no commercial or contracting motion since the 16 Mar LOI offer; the buyer keeps reopening requirements instead of deciding.

deal_risk.text: Amount was cut from $139k to $110k as scope narrowed to core obligation tracking (field history 17 Mar, 14 Apr), a defensive budget signal.
The primary threat is no-decision inertia, not Ironclad: everything is still managed manually today and a higher-priority internal project is competing for attention (10 Feb).

critical_signals.text: The real threat is no-decision inertia, not a vendor: the buyer still tracks everything manually and has reopened requirements three times since 11 May while Ironclad sits as the one active rival in a two-vendor final.

The 29 Apr scenario demo landed on obligation tracking and variations but exposed that the obligation dashboard and escalation are 12 months out on the roadmap, giving the buyer a reason to keep reviewing.
The deal value was cut from $139k to $110k as the buyer narrowed to core CLM, and no commercial motion has happened since the 16 Mar LOI offer.
Nick Middleton, Head of Contract Management and the likely budget owner, has never engaged; the buy is run by Luke Davies, a junior analyst without sign-off authority.

Buyer likely getting defensive on cost or implementation/integration (phased over big-bang) — watch.

commentary.win_position: Win 46 is carried by preference strength +0.40 (weight 20); exec access strength +0.30 (weight 15). Offsetting it: scope narrowed vs prior — Amount cut twice (17 Mar 139k->120k, 14 Apr 120k->110k) as the buyer narrowed to core CLM/obligation tracking….
commentary.deal_momentum: Momentum 52 (roughly flat): Next Step carries 4 dated milestone(s) — actively worked. Silence alone was not treated as backward motion.
commentary.customer_commitment: Commitment sits near the earned-from-zero floor: little observable customer investment surfaced. Commitment is earned, so a low value is honest, not a penalty.
commentary.deal_risk: Risk 32 is driven by Stage is ahead of the engagement/evidence the sweep found; Verdict 'Slowing' — momentum stalling. Only observed negatives count, so these are real warning signs, not gaps.
commentary.forecast_confidence: Forecast confidence 22 is anchored on the stage win (46), nudged by commitment 8 and momentum 52, and dragged by risk 32. Read is Partial Read — a confidence flag (we know less), not a haircut on the score.
commentary.evidence_coverage: Partial Read: 4 of 7 evidence dimensions seen (engagement, stage integrity, exec and champion, forward motion). It labels how much of the picture we have, never the primary scores.

cro_panel.header: Scheme Financial Vehicle — Shortlisted · $110K · closes 31 Aug
cro_panel.intro: Zycus is the buyer-preferred finalist of two on this $110k CLM deal, but the win is stalling on no-decision inertia: three requirement-review delays since 11 May, a budget owner (Nick Middleton) who has never engaged, and the buyer's core asks, an obligation dashboard and escalation, sitting on a 12-month roadmap rather than live.

bullets.text: Amount was cut from $139k to $110k as scope narrowed to core obligation tracking (field history 17 Mar, 14 Apr), a defensive budget signal.
The primary threat is no-decision inertia, not Ironclad: everything is still managed manually today and a higher-priority internal project is competing for attention (10 Feb).

blocks.footer: Competitive picture: The single strongest current threat is no-decision inertia, not a vendor: the buyer runs everything manually today, has a higher-priority internal project (10 Feb) and has reopened requirements three times since 11 May.

blocks.title: Customer commitment
blocks.read: Light — they haven't committed much yet.

bullets.text: Buyer invested in a full RFI/RFP and a sample-data scenario demo (29 Apr) and shortlisted Zycus to the final two, showing genuine intent.
But there has been no commercial or contracting motion since the 16 Mar LOI offer; the buyer keeps reopening requirements instead of deciding.

blocks.title: What moves it forward
items: Offer Luke Davies a joint requirements-closure session to lock ERP integration and escalation scope. (by 13 Jul)
Put the obligation-dashboard and escalation roadmap dates into a written commitment for the buyer. (by 20 Jul)
Ask Luke Davies to broker a working session with Nick Middleton on business case and decision timeline. (by 24 Jul)

evidence_coverage.salesforce_window: trailing 120d activities, 365d field history, full Next Step history (12 Dec 2025 - 2 Jul 2026)
gaps: No quantified business case anywhere across 221 days (breach-penalty and manual-effort cost never sized).
Economic buyer Nick Middleton mapped but never engaged; budget authority unconfirmed.
Only two buyer calls recorded (2 Mar, 29 Apr); no call since 29 Apr, so the last ~68 days are email/Next-Step evidence only.
